HRV stands for heart rate variability — the tiny variation in time between one heartbeat and the next. It's a useful signal for training because it tells you how much your nervous system is recovering from effort, and whether you're ready for harder sessions. Your own HRV is the number of beats per minute that drifts in and out of sync with your breathing, measured overnight. It's a personal metric, so I can't tell you what it is from this context — there's no sensor, no baseline, and no history in your log. The only thing I can do is coach you on how to use it: keep a simple morning resting heart rate logged daily, and once you have a few weeks of readings, we can compare them and see if they drift up or down.

The most plausible driver is last night: you logged 388 minutes, about 6.5 hours, below your 7-day average of 451.6 minutes, and your HRV is suppressed. That is a sleep pattern that is under the baseline, not a bad night. The other inputs are uncorrelated: resting heart rate is 61 bpm, about 0.7 bpm above its 30-day baseline of 60.3 bpm; respiratory rate is 15.3 bpm, close to its usual 14.6 bpm. The recent load is 10.4, comfortably above your 7-day average strain of 12.9. So HRV is suppressed, and the most likely explanation is last night's short, low-duration sleep.
